evidence: Docstring asks 'Are there k consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?' and the header references Wikipedia's Primes_in_arithmetic_progression#Consecutive_primes_in_arithmetic_progression.
The statement is via Set.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength, requiring the set of consecutive primes to be an arithmetic progression.
The progression must consist of consecutive primes (Set.IsPrimeProgressionOfLength built on Nat.Prime).
r_k(N) is defined as the largest subset of {1,...,N} with no non-trivial k-term arithmetic progression, i.e. the maximal progression-free (Salem–Spencer-type) set size.
Asking for an asymptotic formula for r_k(N), the AP-free subset maximum, is the quantitative refinement of Szemerédi's theorem (r_k(N)=o(N)).
The extremal quantity r_k(N) counts subsets containing no non-trivial k-term arithmetic progression (Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard).
The k=3 variant asks the asymptotics of r_3(N), the maximum size of a 3-AP-free (Salem–Spencer) subset of {1,...,N}.
Part (i) asks whether liminf |A ∩ [1,x]|/x = 0, i.e. whether the lower asymptotic density of the well-separated set vanishes.
The problem concerns moments of gaps s_{n+1}-s_n in the sequence of squarefree numbers (Nat.nth Squarefree, OEIS A005117).
The series sum (-1)^n n/p_n runs over p_n, the sequence of primes (k.nth Nat.Prime).
The question is whether the alternating series sum_{n=1}^infty (-1)^n n/p_n converges (Summable of a (-1)^(k+1)-signed sequence).
f(n) is a minimum over all Sidon sets A of size n (IsSidon A); the erdosproblems.com tag is 'sidon sets'.
The counted quantity is isolated elements of the sumset A+A (reference [ESS94] 'On Sum Sets of Sidon Sets').
f(n) ranges over all Sidon sets of size n (IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)); the erdosproblems.com tag is 'sidon sets'.
The mean squared gap is taken over consecutive elements s_1<...<s_t of the sumset A+A (reference [ESS94] 'On Sum Sets of Sidon Sets').
The question is whether A+A is well-distributed over residue classes for Sidon sets A with |A| ~ N^{1/2} (IsSidon hypotheses).
The distributed object is the sumset A+A: 'Must A+A be well-distributed over all small moduli?'.
Lindström's result [Li98] 'Well distribution of Sidon sets in residue classes' concerns the Sidon sets A themselves.
F(N) is the size of the largest Sidon subset of {1,...,N} (Finset.maxSidonSubsetCard) and the question is F(N+k) ≤ F(N)+1.
